1.texto_original: ALEPH. Blessed are they that are perfect in the way, Who walk in the law of Jehovah.
1.normal:
Devotional Study of Psalms 119:1

The first verse establishes the foundation of all biblical revelation: God as sovereign Creator. The phrase "In the beginning" places us at the inaugural moment of creation, not as an event within time, but as the beginning of time itself. God is not part of creation, but its absolute origin.

The verb "created" (from the Hebrew bara) is exclusive to God in the Scriptures, indicating creation ex nihilo (from nothing). This is not about transforming pre-existing matter, but calling into existence what did not exist before. The expression "the heavens and the earth" encompasses the totality of created reality.

This verse answers fundamental questions: Where do we come from? Who is in control? The answer is clear: everything comes from God and depends on Him. As believers, this truth gives us security in divine sovereignty over our lives.

"ALEPH. Blessed are they that are perfect in the way, Who walk in the law of Jehovah."
1.academico:
Exegetical Analysis of Psalms 119:1

Philological Analysis

The original Hebrew text presents crucial elements for exegesis. Bereshit (בְּרֵאשִׁית) is a prepositional construction meaning "in the beginning of", indicating the absolute beginning. The verb bara (בָּרָא) appears in perfect tense (qatal), denoting completed action. This verb has God as its exclusive subject in the Tanakh, distinguishing it from asah (to make) and yatsar (to form).

Historical Context

Source criticism identifies this opening with the Priestly tradition (P), dated to the exilic or post-exilic period (6th-5th century BCE). This contrasts with Mesopotamian creation narratives such as the Enuma Elish (18th century BCE), where creation results from divine conflict.

Theological Implications

The theology of creation establishes: (1) Radical monotheism; (2) Creation ex nihilo; (3) Intrinsic goodness of creation; (4) Absolute sovereignty without cosmic opposition.

Archaeological Evidence

Discoveries at Ugarit (Ras Shamra) and Ebla illuminate Canaanite cosmogonic language, showing how Genesis employs common terms but strips them of mythological connotations.

"ALEPH. Blessed are they that are perfect in the way, Who walk in the law of Jehovah."
1.ninos:
🌌 The Great Beginning! 🌍

Hello, universe explorers! 🚀 Imagine: before there was anything... not even time! ⏰... God decided to CREATE EVERYTHING! ✨

With a powerful "LET THERE BE!" 🤓, God made appear from absolute nothing ✨ the blue skies full of twinkling stars ⭐ and the Earth with mountains 🏔️, rivers 🌊 and animals 🐶. Like the greatest magic ever! 🪄

God is the most amazing artist 🎨 and everything is His masterpiece. Including YOU! 😊 When you see the clouds ☁️ or play in the park 🌳, remember: it all started with Gods creative love! ❤️

"ALEPH. Blessed are they that are perfect in the way, Who walk in the law of Jehovah."

"Thou hast rebuked the proud that are cursed, That do wander from thy commandments."

22.texto_original: Take away from me reproach and contempt; For I have kept thy testimonies.
22.normal:
Devotional Study of Psalms 119:22

The first verse establishes the foundation of all biblical revelation: God as sovereign Creator. The phrase "In the beginning" places us at the inaugural moment of creation, not as an event within time, but as the beginning of time itself. God is not part of creation, but its absolute origin.

The verb "created" (from the Hebrew bara) is exclusive to God in the Scriptures, indicating creation ex nihilo (from nothing). This is not about transforming pre-existing matter, but calling into existence what did not exist before. The expression "the heavens and the earth" encompasses the totality of created reality.

This verse answers fundamental questions: Where do we come from? Who is in control? The answer is clear: everything comes from God and depends on Him. As believers, this truth gives us security in divine sovereignty over our lives.

"Take away from me reproach and contempt; For I have kept thy testimonies."
22.academico:
Exegetical Analysis of Psalms 119:22

Philological Analysis

The original Hebrew text presents crucial elements for exegesis. Bereshit (בְּרֵאשִׁית) is a prepositional construction meaning "in the beginning of", indicating the absolute beginning. The verb bara (בָּרָא) appears in perfect tense (qatal), denoting completed action. This verb has God as its exclusive subject in the Tanakh, distinguishing it from asah (to make) and yatsar (to form).

Historical Context

Source criticism identifies this opening with the Priestly tradition (P), dated to the exilic or post-exilic period (6th-5th century BCE). This contrasts with Mesopotamian creation narratives such as the Enuma Elish (18th century BCE), where creation results from divine conflict.

Theological Implications

The theology of creation establishes: (1) Radical monotheism; (2) Creation ex nihilo; (3) Intrinsic goodness of creation; (4) Absolute sovereignty without cosmic opposition.

Archaeological Evidence

Discoveries at Ugarit (Ras Shamra) and Ebla illuminate Canaanite cosmogonic language, showing how Genesis employs common terms but strips them of mythological connotations.

"Take away from me reproach and contempt; For I have kept thy testimonies."

Discoveries at Ugarit (Ras Shamra) and Ebla illuminate Canaanite cosmogonic language, showing how Genesis employs common terms but strips them of mythological connotations.

God is the most amazing artist 🎨 and everything is His masterpiece. Including YOU! 😊 When you see the clouds ☁️ or play in the park 🌳, remember: it all started with Gods creative love! ❤️

"I have remembered thine ordinances of old, O Jehovah, And have comforted myself."

53.texto_original: Hot indignation hath taken hold upon me, Because of the wicked that forsake thy law.
53.normal:
Devotional Study of Psalms 119:53

The first verse establishes the foundation of all biblical revelation: God as sovereign Creator. The phrase "In the beginning" places us at the inaugural moment of creation, not as an event within time, but as the beginning of time itself. God is not part of creation, but its absolute origin.

The verb "created" (from the Hebrew bara) is exclusive to God in the Scriptures, indicating creation ex nihilo (from nothing). This is not about transforming pre-existing matter, but calling into existence what did not exist before. The expression "the heavens and the earth" encompasses the totality of created reality.

This verse answers fundamental questions: Where do we come from? Who is in control? The answer is clear: everything comes from God and depends on Him. As believers, this truth gives us security in divine sovereignty over our lives.

"Hot indignation hath taken hold upon me, Because of the wicked that forsake thy law."
53.academico:
Exegetical Analysis of Psalms 119:53

Philological Analysis

The original Hebrew text presents crucial elements for exegesis. Bereshit (בְּרֵאשִׁית) is a prepositional construction meaning "in the beginning of", indicating the absolute beginning. The verb bara (בָּרָא) appears in perfect tense (qatal), denoting completed action. This verb has God as its exclusive subject in the Tanakh, distinguishing it from asah (to make) and yatsar (to form).

Historical Context

Source criticism identifies this opening with the Priestly tradition (P), dated to the exilic or post-exilic period (6th-5th century BCE). This contrasts with Mesopotamian creation narratives such as the Enuma Elish (18th century BCE), where creation results from divine conflict.

Theological Implications

The theology of creation establishes: (1) Radical monotheism; (2) Creation ex nihilo; (3) Intrinsic goodness of creation; (4) Absolute sovereignty without cosmic opposition.

Archaeological Evidence

Discoveries at Ugarit (Ras Shamra) and Ebla illuminate Canaanite cosmogonic language, showing how Genesis employs common terms but strips them of mythological connotations.

"Hot indignation hath taken hold upon me, Because of the wicked that forsake thy law."

63.academico:
Exegetical Analysis of Psalms 119:63

Philological Analysis

The original Hebrew text presents crucial elements for exegesis. Bereshit (בְּרֵאשִׁית) is a prepositional construction meaning "in the beginning of", indicating the absolute beginning. The verb bara (בָּרָא) appears in perfect tense (qatal), denoting completed action. This verb has God as its exclusive subject in the Tanakh, distinguishing it from asah (to make) and yatsar (to form).

Historical Context

Source criticism identifies this opening with the Priestly tradition (P), dated to the exilic or post-exilic period (6th-5th century BCE). This contrasts with Mesopotamian creation narratives such as the Enuma Elish (18th century BCE), where creation results from divine conflict.

Theological Implications

The theology of creation establishes: (1) Radical monotheism; (2) Creation ex nihilo; (3) Intrinsic goodness of creation; (4) Absolute sovereignty without cosmic opposition.

Archaeological Evidence

Discoveries at Ugarit (Ras Shamra) and Ebla illuminate Canaanite cosmogonic language, showing how Genesis employs common terms but strips them of mythological connotations.

"I am a companion of all them that fear thee, And of them that observe thy precepts."

74.texto_original: They that fear thee shall see me and be glad, Because I have hoped in thy word.
74.normal:
Devotional Study of Psalms 119:74

The first verse establishes the foundation of all biblical revelation: God as sovereign Creator. The phrase "In the beginning" places us at the inaugural moment of creation, not as an event within time, but as the beginning of time itself. God is not part of creation, but its absolute origin.

The verb "created" (from the Hebrew bara) is exclusive to God in the Scriptures, indicating creation ex nihilo (from nothing). This is not about transforming pre-existing matter, but calling into existence what did not exist before. The expression "the heavens and the earth" encompasses the totality of created reality.

This verse answers fundamental questions: Where do we come from? Who is in control? The answer is clear: everything comes from God and depends on Him. As believers, this truth gives us security in divine sovereignty over our lives.

"They that fear thee shall see me and be glad, Because I have hoped in thy word."
74.academico:
Exegetical Analysis of Psalms 119:74

Philological Analysis

The original Hebrew text presents crucial elements for exegesis. Bereshit (בְּרֵאשִׁית) is a prepositional construction meaning "in the beginning of", indicating the absolute beginning. The verb bara (בָּרָא) appears in perfect tense (qatal), denoting completed action. This verb has God as its exclusive subject in the Tanakh, distinguishing it from asah (to make) and yatsar (to form).

Historical Context

Source criticism identifies this opening with the Priestly tradition (P), dated to the exilic or post-exilic period (6th-5th century BCE). This contrasts with Mesopotamian creation narratives such as the Enuma Elish (18th century BCE), where creation results from divine conflict.

Theological Implications

The theology of creation establishes: (1) Radical monotheism; (2) Creation ex nihilo; (3) Intrinsic goodness of creation; (4) Absolute sovereignty without cosmic opposition.

Archaeological Evidence

Discoveries at Ugarit (Ras Shamra) and Ebla illuminate Canaanite cosmogonic language, showing how Genesis employs common terms but strips them of mythological connotations.

"They that fear thee shall see me and be glad, Because I have hoped in thy word."

84.texto_original: How many are the days of thy servant? When wilt thou execute judgment on them that persecute me?
84.normal:
Devotional Study of Psalms 119:84

The first verse establishes the foundation of all biblical revelation: God as sovereign Creator. The phrase "In the beginning" places us at the inaugural moment of creation, not as an event within time, but as the beginning of time itself. God is not part of creation, but its absolute origin.

The verb "created" (from the Hebrew bara) is exclusive to God in the Scriptures, indicating creation ex nihilo (from nothing). This is not about transforming pre-existing matter, but calling into existence what did not exist before. The expression "the heavens and the earth" encompasses the totality of created reality.

This verse answers fundamental questions: Where do we come from? Who is in control? The answer is clear: everything comes from God and depends on Him. As believers, this truth gives us security in divine sovereignty over our lives.

"How many are the days of thy servant? When wilt thou execute judgment on them that persecute me?"
84.academico:
Exegetical Analysis of Psalms 119:84

Philological Analysis

The original Hebrew text presents crucial elements for exegesis. Bereshit (בְּרֵאשִׁית) is a prepositional construction meaning "in the beginning of", indicating the absolute beginning. The verb bara (בָּרָא) appears in perfect tense (qatal), denoting completed action. This verb has God as its exclusive subject in the Tanakh, distinguishing it from asah (to make) and yatsar (to form).

Historical Context

Source criticism identifies this opening with the Priestly tradition (P), dated to the exilic or post-exilic period (6th-5th century BCE). This contrasts with Mesopotamian creation narratives such as the Enuma Elish (18th century BCE), where creation results from divine conflict.

Theological Implications

The theology of creation establishes: (1) Radical monotheism; (2) Creation ex nihilo; (3) Intrinsic goodness of creation; (4) Absolute sovereignty without cosmic opposition.

Archaeological Evidence

Discoveries at Ugarit (Ras Shamra) and Ebla illuminate Canaanite cosmogonic language, showing how Genesis employs common terms but strips them of mythological connotations.

"How many are the days of thy servant? When wilt thou execute judgment on them that persecute me?"

The original Hebrew text presents crucial elements for exegesis. Bereshit (בְּרֵאשִׁית) is a prepositional construction meaning "in the beginning of", indicating the absolute beginning. The verb bara (בָּרָא) appears in perfect tense (qatal), denoting completed action. This verb has God as its exclusive subject in the Tanakh, distinguishing it from asah (to make) and yatsar (to form).

Historical Context

Source criticism identifies this opening with the Priestly tradition (P), dated to the exilic or post-exilic period (6th-5th century BCE). This contrasts with Mesopotamian creation narratives such as the Enuma Elish (18th century BCE), where creation results from divine conflict.

Theological Implications

The theology of creation establishes: (1) Radical monotheism; (2) Creation ex nihilo; (3) Intrinsic goodness of creation; (4) Absolute sovereignty without cosmic opposition.

Archaeological Evidence

Discoveries at Ugarit (Ras Shamra) and Ebla illuminate Canaanite cosmogonic language, showing how Genesis employs common terms but strips them of mythological connotations.

"I am thine, save me; For I have sought thy precepts."

With a powerful "LET THERE BE!" 🤓, God made appear from absolute nothing ✨ the blue skies full of twinkling stars ⭐ and the Earth with mountains 🏔️, rivers 🌊 and animals 🐶. Like the greatest magic ever! 🪄

God is the most amazing artist 🎨 and everything is His masterpiece. Including YOU! 😊 When you see the clouds ☁️ or play in the park 🌳, remember: it all started with Gods creative love! ❤️

"Through thy precepts I get understanding: Therefore I hate every false way."

105.texto_original: NUN. Thy word is a lamp unto my feet, And light unto my path.
105.normal:
Devotional Study of Psalms 119:105

The first verse establishes the foundation of all biblical revelation: God as sovereign Creator. The phrase "In the beginning" places us at the inaugural moment of creation, not as an event within time, but as the beginning of time itself. God is not part of creation, but its absolute origin.

The verb "created" (from the Hebrew bara) is exclusive to God in the Scriptures, indicating creation ex nihilo (from nothing). This is not about transforming pre-existing matter, but calling into existence what did not exist before. The expression "the heavens and the earth" encompasses the totality of created reality.

This verse answers fundamental questions: Where do we come from? Who is in control? The answer is clear: everything comes from God and depends on Him. As believers, this truth gives us security in divine sovereignty over our lives.

"NUN. Thy word is a lamp unto my feet, And light unto my path."
105.academico:
Exegetical Analysis of Psalms 119:105

Philological Analysis

The original Hebrew text presents crucial elements for exegesis. Bereshit (בְּרֵאשִׁית) is a prepositional construction meaning "in the beginning of", indicating the absolute beginning. The verb bara (בָּרָא) appears in perfect tense (qatal), denoting completed action. This verb has God as its exclusive subject in the Tanakh, distinguishing it from asah (to make) and yatsar (to form).

Historical Context

Source criticism identifies this opening with the Priestly tradition (P), dated to the exilic or post-exilic period (6th-5th century BCE). This contrasts with Mesopotamian creation narratives such as the Enuma Elish (18th century BCE), where creation results from divine conflict.

Theological Implications

The theology of creation establishes: (1) Radical monotheism; (2) Creation ex nihilo; (3) Intrinsic goodness of creation; (4) Absolute sovereignty without cosmic opposition.

Archaeological Evidence

Discoveries at Ugarit (Ras Shamra) and Ebla illuminate Canaanite cosmogonic language, showing how Genesis employs common terms but strips them of mythological connotations.

"NUN. Thy word is a lamp unto my feet, And light unto my path."

The verb "created" (from the Hebrew bara) is exclusive to God in the Scriptures, indicating creation ex nihilo (from nothing). This is not about transforming pre-existing matter, but calling into existence what did not exist before. The expression "the heavens and the earth" encompasses the totality of created reality.

This verse answers fundamental questions: Where do we come from? Who is in control? The answer is clear: everything comes from God and depends on Him. As believers, this truth gives us security in divine sovereignty over our lives.

"Depart from me, ye evil-doers, That I may keep the commandments of my God."
115.academico:
Exegetical Analysis of Psalms 119:115

Philological Analysis

The original Hebrew text presents crucial elements for exegesis. Bereshit (בְּרֵאשִׁית) is a prepositional construction meaning "in the beginning of", indicating the absolute beginning. The verb bara (בָּרָא) appears in perfect tense (qatal), denoting completed action. This verb has God as its exclusive subject in the Tanakh, distinguishing it from asah (to make) and yatsar (to form).

Historical Context

Source criticism identifies this opening with the Priestly tradition (P), dated to the exilic or post-exilic period (6th-5th century BCE). This contrasts with Mesopotamian creation narratives such as the Enuma Elish (18th century BCE), where creation results from divine conflict.

Theological Implications

The theology of creation establishes: (1) Radical monotheism; (2) Creation ex nihilo; (3) Intrinsic goodness of creation; (4) Absolute sovereignty without cosmic opposition.

Archaeological Evidence

Discoveries at Ugarit (Ras Shamra) and Ebla illuminate Canaanite cosmogonic language, showing how Genesis employs common terms but strips them of mythological connotations.

"Depart from me, ye evil-doers, That I may keep the commandments of my God."

God is the most amazing artist 🎨 and everything is His masterpiece. Including YOU! 😊 When you see the clouds ☁️ or play in the park 🌳, remember: it all started with Gods creative love! ❤️

"Make thy face to shine upon thy servant; And teach me thy statutes."

136.texto_original: Streams of water run down mine eyes, Because they observe not thy law.
136.normal:
Devotional Study of Psalms 119:136

The first verse establishes the foundation of all biblical revelation: God as sovereign Creator. The phrase "In the beginning" places us at the inaugural moment of creation, not as an event within time, but as the beginning of time itself. God is not part of creation, but its absolute origin.

The verb "created" (from the Hebrew bara) is exclusive to God in the Scriptures, indicating creation ex nihilo (from nothing). This is not about transforming pre-existing matter, but calling into existence what did not exist before. The expression "the heavens and the earth" encompasses the totality of created reality.

This verse answers fundamental questions: Where do we come from? Who is in control? The answer is clear: everything comes from God and depends on Him. As believers, this truth gives us security in divine sovereignty over our lives.

"Streams of water run down mine eyes, Because they observe not thy law."
136.academico:
Exegetical Analysis of Psalms 119:136

Philological Analysis

The original Hebrew text presents crucial elements for exegesis. Bereshit (בְּרֵאשִׁית) is a prepositional construction meaning "in the beginning of", indicating the absolute beginning. The verb bara (בָּרָא) appears in perfect tense (qatal), denoting completed action. This verb has God as its exclusive subject in the Tanakh, distinguishing it from asah (to make) and yatsar (to form).

Historical Context

Source criticism identifies this opening with the Priestly tradition (P), dated to the exilic or post-exilic period (6th-5th century BCE). This contrasts with Mesopotamian creation narratives such as the Enuma Elish (18th century BCE), where creation results from divine conflict.

Theological Implications

The theology of creation establishes: (1) Radical monotheism; (2) Creation ex nihilo; (3) Intrinsic goodness of creation; (4) Absolute sovereignty without cosmic opposition.

Archaeological Evidence

Discoveries at Ugarit (Ras Shamra) and Ebla illuminate Canaanite cosmogonic language, showing how Genesis employs common terms but strips them of mythological connotations.

"Streams of water run down mine eyes, Because they observe not thy law."

Exegetical Analysis of Psalms 119:146

Philological Analysis

The original Hebrew text presents crucial elements for exegesis. Bereshit (בְּרֵאשִׁית) is a prepositional construction meaning "in the beginning of", indicating the absolute beginning. The verb bara (בָּרָא) appears in perfect tense (qatal), denoting completed action. This verb has God as its exclusive subject in the Tanakh, distinguishing it from asah (to make) and yatsar (to form).

Historical Context

Source criticism identifies this opening with the Priestly tradition (P), dated to the exilic or post-exilic period (6th-5th century BCE). This contrasts with Mesopotamian creation narratives such as the Enuma Elish (18th century BCE), where creation results from divine conflict.

Theological Implications

The theology of creation establishes: (1) Radical monotheism; (2) Creation ex nihilo; (3) Intrinsic goodness of creation; (4) Absolute sovereignty without cosmic opposition.

Archaeological Evidence

Discoveries at Ugarit (Ras Shamra) and Ebla illuminate Canaanite cosmogonic language, showing how Genesis employs common terms but strips them of mythological connotations.

"I have called unto thee; Save me, And I shall observe thy testimonies."

Hello, universe explorers! 🚀 Imagine: before there was anything... not even time! ⏰... God decided to CREATE EVERYTHING! ✨

With a powerful "LET THERE BE!" 🤓, God made appear from absolute nothing ✨ the blue skies full of twinkling stars ⭐ and the Earth with mountains 🏔️, rivers 🌊 and animals 🐶. Like the greatest magic ever! 🪄

God is the most amazing artist 🎨 and everything is His masterpiece. Including YOU! 😊 When you see the clouds ☁️ or play in the park 🌳, remember: it all started with Gods creative love! ❤️

"Great are thy tender mercies, O Jehovah: Quicken me according to thine ordinances."

157.texto_original: Many are my persecutors and mine adversaries; [Yet] have I not swerved from thy testimonies.
157.normal:
Devotional Study of Psalms 119:157

The first verse establishes the foundation of all biblical revelation: God as sovereign Creator. The phrase "In the beginning" places us at the inaugural moment of creation, not as an event within time, but as the beginning of time itself. God is not part of creation, but its absolute origin.

The verb "created" (from the Hebrew bara) is exclusive to God in the Scriptures, indicating creation ex nihilo (from nothing). This is not about transforming pre-existing matter, but calling into existence what did not exist before. The expression "the heavens and the earth" encompasses the totality of created reality.

This verse answers fundamental questions: Where do we come from? Who is in control? The answer is clear: everything comes from God and depends on Him. As believers, this truth gives us security in divine sovereignty over our lives.

"Many are my persecutors and mine adversaries; [Yet] have I not swerved from thy testimonies."
157.academico:
Exegetical Analysis of Psalms 119:157

Philological Analysis

The original Hebrew text presents crucial elements for exegesis. Bereshit (בְּרֵאשִׁית) is a prepositional construction meaning "in the beginning of", indicating the absolute beginning. The verb bara (בָּרָא) appears in perfect tense (qatal), denoting completed action. This verb has God as its exclusive subject in the Tanakh, distinguishing it from asah (to make) and yatsar (to form).

Historical Context

Source criticism identifies this opening with the Priestly tradition (P), dated to the exilic or post-exilic period (6th-5th century BCE). This contrasts with Mesopotamian creation narratives such as the Enuma Elish (18th century BCE), where creation results from divine conflict.

Theological Implications

The theology of creation establishes: (1) Radical monotheism; (2) Creation ex nihilo; (3) Intrinsic goodness of creation; (4) Absolute sovereignty without cosmic opposition.

Archaeological Evidence

Discoveries at Ugarit (Ras Shamra) and Ebla illuminate Canaanite cosmogonic language, showing how Genesis employs common terms but strips them of mythological connotations.

"Many are my persecutors and mine adversaries; [Yet] have I not swerved from thy testimonies."

This is not about transforming pre-existing matter, but calling into existence what did not exist before. The expression "the heavens and the earth" encompasses the totality of created reality.

This verse answers fundamental questions: Where do we come from? Who is in control? The answer is clear: everything comes from God and depends on Him. As believers, this truth gives us security in divine sovereignty over our lives.

"My soul hath observed thy testimonies; And I love them exceedingly."
167.academico:
Exegetical Analysis of Psalms 119:167

Philological Analysis

The original Hebrew text presents crucial elements for exegesis. Bereshit (בְּרֵאשִׁית) is a prepositional construction meaning "in the beginning of", indicating the absolute beginning. The verb bara (בָּרָא) appears in perfect tense (qatal), denoting completed action. This verb has God as its exclusive subject in the Tanakh, distinguishing it from asah (to make) and yatsar (to form).

Historical Context

Source criticism identifies this opening with the Priestly tradition (P), dated to the exilic or post-exilic period (6th-5th century BCE). This contrasts with Mesopotamian creation narratives such as the Enuma Elish (18th century BCE), where creation results from divine conflict.

Theological Implications

The theology of creation establishes: (1) Radical monotheism; (2) Creation ex nihilo; (3) Intrinsic goodness of creation; (4) Absolute sovereignty without cosmic opposition.

Archaeological Evidence

Discoveries at Ugarit (Ras Shamra) and Ebla illuminate Canaanite cosmogonic language, showing how Genesis employs common terms but strips them of mythological connotations.

"My soul hath observed thy testimonies; And I love them exceedingly."
